old_claim.note: Public Notice Boards provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Open public market spaces were the historical sale venue that enclosed seventeenth-century auction houses formalized.

source_shape.source_locator: Historical overview section: auctions used to sell assets and liquidate debtor assets in many facilities and open-air public spaces before enclosed showroom auction houses began in the 17th century, with Stockholms Auktionsverk founded in 1674.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places enclosed auction houses after older public-space auction practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The corrected edge points to public market/sale venues rather than notice boards, because the source-backed transition is from open public sale spaces to enclosed auction houses.

ontology_before: Commercial Auction Houses depended on public notice boards, implying the key predecessor was posted notice infrastructure.
ontology_after: Commercial Auction Houses depend on town market squares as the older public sale venue that enclosed auction houses formalized.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: commercial_auction_houses now depends on town_market_squares and no longer directly depends on public_notice_boards.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to auction notices or advertising rather than auction-house venues and sale operations.
A stronger source shows Stockholm's 1674 auction-house system directly required posted public notice boards.
A dedicated auction-notice or auction-catalog node is added and becomes the more precise intermediary.
short_reason: Auction houses formalized public sale venues; notice boards are adjacent publicity infrastructure.
